Where bent-up bars are provided, their contribution to the total shear resistance must not exceed half the total — the rest must come from stirrups. The shear that the reinforcement must carry is Vus = Vu − τc·b·d, where Vu is the design shear and τc·b·d is the contribution of the concrete (using b = bw for flanged sections). The strength contributed by the shear reinforcement Vus is calculated by one of three formulae depending on the configuration:
(a) For vertical stirrups: Vus = 0.87·fy·Asv·d / sv.
(b) For inclined stirrups, or for a series of bars bent-up at different cross-sections: Vus = (0.87·fy·Asv·d / sv) × (sin α + cos α).
(c) For a single bar or a single group of parallel bars all bent-up at the same cross-section: Vus = 0.87·fy·Asv·sin α.

In all three formulae: Asv is the total cross-sectional area of stirrup legs (or bent-up bars) within a length sv along the member; sv is the spacing of the stirrups (or bent-up bars) along the member length; fy is the characteristic strength of the stirrup or bent-up reinforcement, capped at 415 N/mm² regardless of the actual grade specified; b is the breadth (bw for flanged sections); d is the effective depth; and α is the angle between the inclined stirrup or bent-up bar and the axis of the member, which must not be less than 45 degrees. The recurring 0.87·fy multiplier is the design steel stress fy/γm with γm = 1.15.

NOTE 1: where more than one type of shear reinforcement is used in the same portion of a beam, the total shear resistance is computed as the sum of the resistances for the various types calculated separately. NOTE 2: independent of the design Vus, the stirrup area must not be less than the minimum prescribed in 26.5.1.6.
formula_nl_summary: When τv > τc, design shear reinforcement to carry Vus = Vu − τc·b·d. Three configurations: vertical stirrups Vus = 0.87·fy·Asv·d/sv; inclined stirrups or series of bent-up bars at different sections Vus = 0.87·fy·Asv·d·(sin α + cos α)/sv; single group of bent-up bars at one section Vus = 0.87·fy·Asv·sin α. Constraints: bent-up bars contribute at most 50% of total Vus, fy ≤ 415 N/mm², α ≥ 45°, total stirrups ≥ 26.5.1.6 minimum, multiple types sum independently.

title: Enhanced shear strength near supports — general principle
text:
Shear failure in beams and cantilevers without shear reinforcement normally occurs along a plane inclined at about 30 degrees to the horizontal. When the section under consideration is closer to a support or concentrated load than twice the effective depth, the failure plane is geometrically constrained to be more steeply inclined than this 30-degree default, and the shear force required to produce failure is correspondingly increased. The code allows this enhancement to be taken into account by raising the design shear strength of concrete from the Table 19 value τc up to 2d·τc/av, where av is the horizontal distance from the face of the support to the section being considered (or to the concentrated load), provided av ≤ 2d. Beyond a distance of 2d from the support, the conventional Table 19 value applies unchanged.

Two provisos guard the enhancement. First, the design shear stress at the face of the support itself must remain less than τc,max from Table 20 — the cap on diagonal-strut crushing applies regardless of how close the section is to the support, since steel reinforcement cannot relieve concrete crushing. Second, for the enhancement to develop physically, the tension reinforcement at the section must extend on each side of the point where the possible failure plane intersects it for a distance at least equal to the effective depth d, or be provided with an equivalent anchorage device. Without this anchorage continuity, the tension chord of the truss model is not present to balance the steeper compression strut, and the enhanced shear capacity cannot be mobilised. Fig 24 in the code illustrates the geometry — failure plane (X − X) inclined at angle θ from a support face, with av measured from the face to the section.
formula_nl_summary: Within 2d of a support face, the design shear strength of concrete may be enhanced from τc (Table 19) to 2d·τc/av, where av is the horizontal distance from the support face to the section. The enhancement is capped by τc,max (Table 20) at the support face. Tension reinforcement must extend at least d on each side of the failure-plane intersection (or have equivalent anchorage) for the enhancement to be effective.

title: Shear failure near supports
nl_caption: Geometry diagram for the enhanced-shear-strength provision of clause 40.5.1. The figure shows the end region of a beam: a hatched support at the bottom-left; uniformly distributed downward load arrows along the top fibre; a failure plane drawn as a solid inclined line rising from the inner face of the support up to a point on the top fibre, inclined at an angle θ from the horizontal; and the horizontal distance av measured along the beam axis from the face of the support to the cross-section X − X where the failure plane meets the top fibre. The accompanying NOTE clarifies that the shear causing failure is the shear acting on the X − X cross-section. The diagram visualises why the failure plane being forced steeper (smaller av, larger θ) increases the apparent concrete shear capacity — the corresponding 2d·τc/av enhancement formula in 40.5.1 is the algebraic statement of this geometric effect.

title: Shear reinforcement for sections close to supports
text:
Where shear reinforcement is required at a section closer than 2d to a support face, its total cross-sectional area As (note: this is the total area placed near the support, not the per-spacing stirrup area Asv used in 40.4) is given by As = av·b·(τv − 2d·τc/av) / (0.87·fy), with the floor As ≥ 0.4·av·b / (0.87·fy). Here av is the horizontal distance from the support face to the section, b is the breadth of the member (use bw for flanged sections per 40.1), τv is the nominal shear stress at the section computed per 40.1, τc is the un-enhanced Table 19 value (the formula already encodes the 2d/av enhancement via the τv − 2d·τc/av term), and fy is the steel strength capped at 415 N/mm². The 0.87·fy denominator is the design yield stress fy/γm with γm = 1.15. Note carefully the As / Asv notation collision with 40.4 — the rules layer should keep these as distinct quantities: As here is a one-shot total over a region near the support; Asv in 40.4 is the per-spacing leg area along the member.

The reinforcement should be distributed within the middle three-quarters of av (i.e., centred on the failure plane intersection, leaving the outer eighth at each end bare). Where av is less than the effective depth d (very-short shear span), horizontal shear reinforcement is more effective than vertical — this reflects the physical geometry: with av < d, the failure plane is steeper than 45 degrees, so reinforcement aligned closer to the horizontal crosses it at a more favourable angle and resists the diagonal tension more directly.
formula_nl_summary: When av < 2d and shear reinforcement is needed: total area As = av·b·(τv − 2d·τc/av)/(0.87·fy), with floor As ≥ 0.4·av·b/(0.87·fy). As here is the total area placed near the support — distinct from the per-spacing Asv of 40.4. Distribute within the middle 3/4 of av. For av < d, horizontal shear reinforcement outperforms vertical because the steeper failure plane geometry favours horizontal-aligned steel.

title: Enhanced shear strength near supports — simplified approach
text:
While the rigorous procedure of 40.5.1 and 40.5.2 may be applied to any beam, IS 456 permits a simplified approach for two common cases: beams carrying generally uniform load (e.g. self-weight, slab loads transferred as line load), and beams where the principal concentrated load is located farther than 2d from the face of the support. In these cases, the shear stress need only be calculated at a single section located a distance d from the face of the support, rather than at the support face or at multiple sections within 2d. The design shear strength τc is read from Table 19 in the conventional way — there is no 2d·τc/av enhancement in this simplified approach, because the d-distance critical section already accounts implicitly for the nearby-support effect. Appropriate shear reinforcement is then designed at this critical section per 40.4 (or minimum reinforcement per 40.3 if τv ≤ τc), and the same arrangement is provided at all sections closer to the support — no further shear check is required at sections between the support face and the d-distance section.

This simplified approach is the one most commonly used in design practice for simply-supported beams under distributed load, and is conventionally called 'critical section at d'. It is conservative compared to the full 2d·τc/av enhancement of 40.5.1 (which would permit a smaller stirrup quantity by raising τc), and trades some economy for procedural simplicity. It mirrors 41.2's simplified rule for the critical section under torsion.
formula_nl_summary: Simplified rule for enhanced shear strength near supports: applicable to beams under uniform load, or with the principal point load located farther than 2d from the support. Take the critical section at a distance d from the support face. Compute τv there, look up τc from Table 19 with no 2d/av enhancement, design shear reinforcement at that section per 40.4, and extend the same arrangement to the support face with no further shear check inside d.

title: Torsion — general scope and design philosophy
text:
Section 41 distinguishes two types of torsion in indeterminate structures. The first is **equilibrium torsion**, where the torsional moment is required to maintain global equilibrium of the structure (e.g. a beam supporting an eccentric balcony cantilever; a spandrel beam with edge slabs framing into one side only; a curved beam in plan). For equilibrium torsion, members must be designed for the torsional moment in accordance with 41.2 (critical section), 41.3 (combined shear and torsion via equivalent shear), and 41.4 (longitudinal and transverse reinforcement). The second type is **compatibility torsion**, where torsion arises only from rotational restraint between members at a joint and can be released without compromising equilibrium (e.g. a secondary beam framing into the side of a primary beam — if the primary beam is allowed to twist, the secondary beam's end fixity moment vanishes). For compatibility torsion, no specific torsional design is required, provided torsional stiffness is set to zero in the structural analysis (releasing the restraint). Adequate control of any incidental torsional cracking is then assumed to be provided by the shear reinforcement designed under Section 40.

The design philosophy adopted in 41.3 and 41.4 (and clarified in the NOTE under 41.1) is the **fictitious-action approach**: rather than designing torsion reinforcement separately from bending and shear reinforcement, the actual bending moment is replaced by a larger fictitious bending moment that accounts for torsion (Me1, defined in 41.4.2), and the actual shear is replaced by a larger fictitious equivalent shear that accounts for torsion (Ve, defined in 41.3.1). Conventional flexure and shear design then proceeds with these augmented actions; no separate torsion check appears as an independent step.

Per 41.1.1, the design rules of 41.3 and 41.4 are written for solid rectangular cross-sections, but may be applied to flanged beams (T-sections, L-sections, etc.) by substituting bw, the breadth of the web, in place of b. This substitution is generally conservative — it ignores any contribution of the flanges to torsional stiffness and resistance — and specialist literature should be consulted where greater accuracy is needed.
formula_nl_summary: Section 41 covers torsion design. Equilibrium torsion (required for global equilibrium) → design per 41.2/41.3/41.4. Compatibility torsion (joint-restraint only) → may be released by zeroing torsional stiffness in analysis; controlled by Section 40 shear reinforcement. Design philosophy is the fictitious-action approach: torsion is folded into augmented bending moment Me1 (41.4.2) and augmented equivalent shear Ve (41.3.1). 41.1.1: rules apply to solid rectangular sections; flanged beams use bw conservatively.

title: Critical section for torsion design
text: The critical section for torsion design is taken at a distance d (the effective depth) from the face of the support. Sections closer to the support than d need not be designed for the higher torsion that the analysis produces in that interval; instead, they may be designed for the same torsional moment Tu as the critical section located at d. This rule mirrors the analogous simplified shear rule of 40.5.3 (critical section at d for shear under uniform load) and reflects the physical fact that the support reaction itself does not generate torsion at the section being checked, so the torsional demand peaks just outside the support reaction zone, not at the face. The d-distance critical section is therefore the relevant location at which to evaluate both Tu and Vu for the equivalent-shear formula of 41.3.1 and the equivalent-moment formula of 41.4.2.
formula_nl_summary: Critical section for torsion design: take Tu as computed at a distance d from the support face. Sections within d of the support are designed for the same torsion as the critical section. Mirrors the simplified shear rule of 40.5.3.

title: Equivalent shear for combined shear and torsion
text:
For sections subjected to combined shear and torsion, an **equivalent shear** Ve is computed that bundles the actual shear Vu with a torsion-derived contribution: Ve = Vu + 1.6·Tu/b, where Tu is the design torsional moment, Vu is the design shear from analysis, and b is the breadth of the beam (use bw for flanged sections per 41.1.1). The factor 1.6/b converts the torsional moment into an equivalent shear force per the IS 456 fictitious-action philosophy of 41.1 — the torsional shear flow circulating around the section is approximated as a uniform shear of magnitude 1.6·Tu/b acting alongside the actual transverse shear.

The **equivalent nominal shear stress** τve is then computed using the formula of 40.1 with Vu replaced by Ve: τve = Ve / (b·d) for uniform-depth beams, or the varying-depth analogue with Ve substituted for Vu. This τve is the quantity then compared against τc (Table 19) and τc,max (Table 20) in 41.3.2 and 41.3.3 to decide on minimum or designed reinforcement.

The cap is hard: τve must not exceed τc,max from Table 20. Exceeding it means the section has insufficient capacity in combined shear-and-torsion regardless of how much transverse reinforcement is added, and must be redesigned (larger section or higher concrete grade). This parallels the τv ≤ τc,max cap for pure shear (40.2.3) and reflects the same physical limit — crushing of the diagonal compression strut in the truss model.
formula_nl_summary: For combined shear and torsion: equivalent shear Ve = Vu + 1.6·Tu/b. Equivalent nominal shear stress τve is computed as in 40.1 with Vu replaced by Ve. τve must not exceed τc,max from Table 20 — same hard cap as for pure shear, exceeding it requires redesign. Use bw for flanged sections.

title: Minimum shear reinforcement when τve ≤ τc
text: When the equivalent nominal shear stress τve (computed from 41.3.1) does not exceed the design shear strength of concrete τc (from Table 19, indexed by 100·As/(b·d) and concrete grade), the section's combined shear-and-torsion demand is small enough that the concrete alone can carry it. In that case, only minimum shear reinforcement is required, per the detailing rule of clause 26.5.1.6. This mirrors the pure-shear case of 40.3, with τv replaced by τve. The minimum-reinforcement provision again provides ductility and crack-control rather than design shear capacity per se, and the longitudinal reinforcement is not augmented above what flexural design alone requires.
formula_nl_summary: When τve ≤ τc (Table 19), provide minimum shear reinforcement per 26.5.1.6. Same rule structure as the pure-shear case 40.3 with τv replaced by τve. No separately designed transverse or longitudinal reinforcement required for torsion in this range.

title: Designed reinforcement when τve > τc — both longitudinal and transverse
text: When the equivalent nominal shear stress τve exceeds the design shear strength of concrete τc (Table 19), the section's combined shear-and-torsion demand exceeds what the concrete alone can carry. In that case, BOTH longitudinal and transverse reinforcement must be designed per clause 41.4 — the longitudinal reinforcement per 41.4.2 (sized for the equivalent bending moment Me1 = Mu + Mt that augments the actual bending moment with a torsion contribution) and the transverse reinforcement per 41.4.3 (closed hoops sized for combined Tu and Vu effects, with a floor based on the surplus stress (τve − τc)). This contrasts with the pure-shear case of 40.4, where only transverse reinforcement (stirrups or bent-up bars) is designed; torsion uniquely requires longitudinal augmentation in addition to transverse, because the torsional shear flow demands a continuous tension chord on all four sides of the section.
formula_nl_summary: When τve > τc (Table 19): provide BOTH longitudinal reinforcement (per 41.4.2, for equivalent moment Me1) and transverse reinforcement (per 41.4.3, two-legged closed hoops sized for combined Tu and Vu). Differs from pure-shear (40.4) in requiring longitudinal augmentation in addition to transverse — the defining feature of torsion design.

title: Torsion reinforcement — composition
text: Where torsion-induced reinforcement is required (i.e. where 41.3.3 has triggered, with τve > τc), the reinforcement consists of two distinct components designed in tandem: longitudinal reinforcement, sized per 41.4.2 to resist an equivalent bending moment Me1 = Mu + Mt that bundles the actual bending with a torsion contribution; and transverse reinforcement, sized per 41.4.3, taking the form of two-legged closed hoops enclosing the corner longitudinal bars, with the area Asv designed for combined torsional moment Tu and shear Vu and bounded below by a floor based on the surplus shear stress (τve − τc). Both components are mandatory when 41.3.3 triggers — neither alone is sufficient, because the torsional shear flow circulates around the entire perimeter of the section and demands closed-loop continuity in both axial and circumferential reinforcement.
formula_nl_summary: When torsion reinforcement is required (per 41.3.3), it consists of two parts: longitudinal reinforcement (41.4.2, sized for Me1 = Mu + Mt) AND transverse reinforcement (41.4.3, two-legged closed hoops sized for combined Tu and Vu). Both required; neither alone is sufficient.

title: Longitudinal reinforcement for torsion — equivalent bending moment Me1 (and Me2 for high-torsion case)
text:
The longitudinal reinforcement at a section subjected to combined bending and torsion is designed to resist an **equivalent bending moment** Me1 that bundles the actual bending moment with a torsion contribution: Me1 = Mu + Mt. Here Mu is the bending moment at the cross-section from analysis, and Mt is the torsion-equivalent bending moment Mt = Tu·(1 + D/b) / 1.7. Tu is the design torsional moment, D is the overall depth of the beam, and b is the breadth (use bw for flanged sections per 41.1.1). The 1.7 divisor and the (1 + D/b) shape factor convert the torsional moment into an equivalent bending action per the fictitious-action philosophy of 41.1. Conventional flexural design under the limit state of collapse is then carried out for Me1 — calculate the required longitudinal reinforcement using the assumptions of 38.1, with Mu replaced by Me1 throughout.

Clause 41.4.2.1 adds a second case for sections where torsion dominates over bending. If the magnitude of Mt exceeds the magnitude of Mu, additional longitudinal reinforcement must be provided on the **flexural compression face** of the beam to resist a second equivalent moment Me2 = Mt − Mu, taken as acting in the opposite sense to Mu. Physically, this captures the fact that under high torsion the section can experience reverse curvature: the face that would normally be in compression under pure bending is instead in tension under the dominant torsional component, and longitudinal steel is required there to resist tension cracking. The compression-face reinforcement is sized by treating the section as a flexural member resisting Me2, with the original tension face now playing the role of the compression face.
formula_nl_summary: Longitudinal reinforcement for torsion: design for equivalent bending moment Me1 = Mu + Mt, where Mt = Tu·(1 + D/b)/1.7. If Mt > Mu (torsion dominates), additionally provide longitudinal reinforcement on the flexural compression face for Me2 = Mt − Mu, acting opposite to Mu — captures the reverse-curvature effect under high torsion. Use bw for flanged sections.

title: Transverse reinforcement for torsion — closed hoops with combined Tu+Vu sizing
text:
Transverse reinforcement at a section subjected to combined shear and torsion takes the form of **two-legged closed hoops enclosing the corner longitudinal bars** — closed hoops are required (rather than open stirrups) because the torsional shear flow circulates around the entire perimeter of the section and demands a continuous tension chord on all four sides. The required area of cross-section Asv per pair of legs at spacing sv is the sum of two contributions, one for torsion and one for shear:

Asv = Tu·sv / (b1·d1·0.87·fy)  +  Vu·sv / (2.5·d1·0.87·fy)

Here Tu is the design torsional moment; Vu is the design shear force at the section; sv is the spacing of the closed hoops along the member; b1 is the centre-to-centre distance between corner bars in the direction of the width; d1 is the centre-to-centre distance between corner bars in the depth direction; and the recurring 0.87·fy is the design yield stress of the hoop steel (with fy ≤ 415 N/mm² as for all shear and torsion reinforcement). The first term resolves the torsional shear flow into a tension demand on each pair of hoop legs (the b1·d1 product is the cross-sectional area of the closed-hoop circuit, by the thin-walled tube analogy); the second term resolves the conventional vertical shear into hoop tension, with the 2.5·d1 lever-arm reflecting the smaller depth available for the truss model when the corner-to-corner distance d1 is used in place of the effective depth d.

The sum above is the design Asv. However, regardless of the design value, the total transverse reinforcement must not be less than the floor (τve − τc)·b·sv / (0.87·fy), where the breadth in the floor formula is b (the full breadth of the member), not b1 — the notation distinction is deliberate. The floor is the area required to carry the surplus shear stress (τve − τc) over a strip of width b and length sv, by the same logic as the pure-shear formula of 40.4. The provided Asv is the maximum of the design value and the floor: Asv,provided = max(design, floor). Note that 41.3.1's cap τve ≤ τc,max from Table 20 still applies — if exceeded, this clause's reinforcement formulas cannot rescue the section and a redesign (larger section or higher concrete grade) is required.
formula_nl_summary: Transverse reinforcement for combined shear and torsion: two-legged closed hoops enclosing the corner longitudinal bars. Asv per pair (at spacing sv) = Tu·sv/(b1·d1·0.87·fy) + Vu·sv/(2.5·d1·0.87·fy). Floor: Asv ≥ (τve − τc)·b·sv/(0.87·fy). Provided value is max(design, floor). b1 is corner-to-corner in width direction; d1 is corner-to-corner in depth direction; b is full member breadth (only in the floor formula); fy ≤ 415 N/mm². τve capped at τc,max (Table 20) — exceeding it requires redesign.
